elements.sent-2411-001: The fifth generation (5G) of mobile communications relies on extremely high data transmissions using a large variety of frequency bands, such as FR1 (sub-6\:GHz) and FR2~(mmWave).
elements.sent-2411-002: Future mobile communications envisage using electromagnetic spectrum beyond FR2, i.e. above 100\:GHz, \blue{known as sub-THz band.}
elements.sent-2411-003: T hese new \blue{frequencies open up challenging scenarios where communications \blue{shall rely on a major contribution such as the \blue{l ine-of-\blue{s ight~(LoS) component.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: To the best of the authors' knowledge, for the first time in literature this work \blue{studies the human blockage \blue{effects over an extremely wide frequency band from 75\:GHz to 215\:GHz given\blue{: (i) the distance between the \blue{blocker and the antennas and \blue{(ii) the \blue{body orientation .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, \blue{the obtained results are modeled with the classical path loss models and compared to 3GPP \blue{alternatives .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The average losses increase from 42\:dB to~56\:dB when frequency rises from 75\:GHz to 215\:GHz.
elements.sent-2411-007: In terms of distance, a 18\:dB increment in the received power is found when the Tx--Rx \blue{separation is increased from 1\:m to 2.5\:m.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Systems operating at low frequencies (sub-6\:GHz) can rely on the main propagation mechanisms, such as reflection, diffraction, and scattering \cite{Andersen1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In contrast, this is not the case for sub-THz and THz bands.
elements.sent-2411-010: At these frequencies, the electrical dimensions of the objects increase considerably producing a detriment of diffraction effects.
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, diffuse scattering becomes more relevant due to the surface roughness.
elements.sent-2411-012: These behaviors, in addition to the reduced aperture size of antennas in the THz region, involve that communications must rely on the line-of-sight (LoS) component~\cite{Guan2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In mobile communication scenarios, this contribution can be shadowed occasionally.
elements.sent-2411-014: Since the radius of the first Fresnel zone is proportional to the wavelength, the human body can easily block the LoS.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, accurate deterministic human body shadowing channel models are needed.
elements.sent-2411-016: Some examples of these models have been proposed at mmWave and THz bands in the state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, in \cite{Karadimas2013 the received signal power variations are statistically characterized in the time domain caused by human activity affecting 60\:GHz indoor short-range wireless links.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In~\cite{Dalveren2021 , the authors provide a simple approach to characterize the effects of scattering by human bodies in the vicinity of a short-range indoor link at 28\:GHz, while the link is fully blocked by another body.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In \cite{Virk2020 , human blockage measurements in an anechoic chamber at 15\:GHz, 28\:GHz, and 60\:GHz frequencies employing 15 human subjects of different sizes and weights are presented and an effective 3-D human blockage model as a \ac{DTMKE scheme is also proposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Indeed, diffraction has been used in many studies to model human shadowing.
elements.sent-2411-021: Knife-edge diffraction (KED) \cite{ITU2019 , \cite{MacCartney2016 models are commonly used in literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, it has the drawback that the 3D models are limited to a \blue{geometry based on metallic planes .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, the scattering path from the human body cannot be separated from the LoS path.
elements.sent-2411-024: The \ac{UTD \cite{Pathak1980 features moderate computational cost and a flexible choice of 3D models including screens, hexagon cylinders, and circular cylinders.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Physical optics~(PO) approximation features a good accuracy for considering the most detailed circumference of the cross-section (CCS) as a human phantom, but the computational cost is \blue{unacceptable for dynamic scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Most of the studies focus on mmWave, and some others are increasing the frequency to sub-THz bands.
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, in \cite{Takagi2022 authors present the results of the evaluation of reflection and blockage losses due to the human body in the 300\:GHz~band.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this work, a study on human blockage of the LoS is performed in \blue{a large bandwidth from 75\:GHz to 215\:GHz \blue{including both mmWave and sub-THz frequencies.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: After a thorough analysis in terms of frequency, distance between the antennas, blocker orientation and human subject; the blockage gain parameter is proposed to evaluate the impact on the LoS signal.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then, it is modeled applying a modified version of classical path loss models as function of frequency and distance alternatively to the geometrical based models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Accurate results are obtained, with reduced standard deviation and high channel predictability.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, our proposal is compared with the geometrical models from the \ac{3GPP known as 3GPP-A and 3GPP-B~\cite{3GPP2022 , \blue{implying higher complexity and less accurate results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Quantify the blockage effects in the sub-THz and THz bands is fundamental on the development of personal area network~(PAN) and body area network (BAN) systems in future cellular generations.
elements.sent-2411-034: The \blue{rest of the article is organized as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Section~II presents the measurements \blue{acquisition process and their post-processing.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Section III analyzes the influence of the \blue{frequency, distance between the antennas, blocker orientation and human subject.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Then , it presents \blue{the applied models and their fit with the experimental results.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A comparison with other models from the literature is \blue{carried out .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Finally, Section~IV exposes the conclusions of this work.
elements.sent-2411-040: Measurements have been carried out in a laboratory located at the facilities of Universidad Politécnica de Cartagena~(UPCT), with a volume of 8\:$\times$\:4.8\:$\times$\:3.5\:m\textsuperscript{3 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The \blue{scenario is furnished with desks, chairs, shelves and closets; and it is equipped with \blue{several electronic devices such as computers and measurement instruments \blue{(see Fig. \ref{fig:setup_image ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx) are aligned facing each other at a distance \blue{$d$\:$\in$\:\{1, 1.75, 2.5\}\:m and at a height above the ground $h_c$\:=\:1\:m.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The Tx and Rx antennas are standard gain horns (SGHs) of 20\:dB gain and a half-power beam width (HPBW) of 18º in H and E planes at mid-band (Flann SGH Series 240 \#27240 and \#29240).
elements.sent-2411-044: The VNA is configured in continuous wave (CW) mode, with a sampling frequency of 100\:Hz (time step of 10\:ms).
elements.sent-2411-045: The number of points is set to 2\:048, which provides a maximum excess time of 20.47\:s.
elements.sent-2411-046: The most restrictive dynamic range is above 80\:dB, \blue{and it is observed for the highest frequency, i.e. 215\:GHz.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Two subjects participated in the measurements, performing two blockages crossing head-on and another two in sideways per frequency and distance.
elements.sent-2411-048: Therefore, the total number of captured blockages is 216.
elements.sent-2411-049: At each one, the crossing speed was constant and took a value of 45\:cm/s, i.e. 1.62\:km/h.
elements.sent-2411-050: It \blue{was lower than the typical values for pedestrian speed\footnote{\blue{The typical pedestrian speed varies between 4\:km/h and 6\:km/h \cite{Murtagh2020 .}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics{figures/setup.pdf} \caption{\blue{Panoramic of the} measurement setup at UPCT facilities.} \label{fig:setup_image}
elements.sent-2411-051: The magnitude is normalized to the power of the LoS component (including the antennas gain), which can be seen as a continuous value at the beginning and end of the blockage event.
elements.sent-2411-052: At the central part, the LoS component is completely blocked, which implies a degradation of the received power and an increase in fast fading effects.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, several effects such as arm movements (resulting in partial signal recovery) and diffraction plus reflection interferences are observed.
elements.sent-2411-054: In order to characterize the blockage, fast fading effects have to be removed.
elements.sent-2411-055: This can be done applying an averaging over the local mean power with a window size of 36 samples\footnote{Given \blue{a crossing speed of 45\:cm/s, \blue{at the lowest studied frequency the window size is equivalent to 39$\lambda$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This value is within the range from 20$\lambda$ to 40$\lambda$, which retains the trace slow-fading information \cite{Lee1985 .. \cite{Lee1985 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We define it as the minimum gain provided by the human along the blockage after removing the fast fading effects.
elements.sent-2411-058: In Fig. \ref{fig:blockage_example , blockage gain takes a value of $-$45.5\:dB.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics{figures/new_figure2.pdf} \caption{\blue{Example of} blockage \blue{event measured for $f$\:=\:}75\:GHz and \blue{$d$\:=\:}1\:m \blue{(normalized with respect to the LoS component)}. \blue{The red line results from removing the fast fading effects of the measurement.}} \label{fig:blockage_example}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics{figures/new_figure3.pdf} \caption{\blue{Blockage gain distribution per studied frequency (violin diagrams). The fit with the modified floating-intercept model is included as a black line.}} \label{fig:boxplot_freq}
elements.sent-2411-059: Then, it has been analyzed in terms of the factors taken into account in our experiment.
elements.sent-2411-060: As Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq reveals, the BG follows a clear decreasing trend \blue{with frequency.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In particular, median values of $-$43.0\:dB and $-$59.7\:dB are obtained for the lowest and highest frequencies respectively (75\:GHz and 215\:GHz).
elements.sent-2411-062: While the signal attenuation in this frequency range for free space propagation is 9.1\:dB, losses of up to 16.7\:dB are observed with during the blockage event.
elements.sent-2411-063: This is due to the growth of the blocker electrical dimensions, which intrinsically increases the attenuation with frequency scaling.
elements.sent-2411-064: In contrast with \blue{the frequency trend, the blockage gain increases as the distance between the antennas is larger.}
elements.sent-2411-065: At each configuration (1\:m, 1.75\:m, and 2.5\:m), the median values of BG are $-$59.5\:dB, $-$49.4\:dB, and $-$41.2\:dB respectively; i.e., a 18.3\:dB increment from $d$\:=\:1\:m to $d$\:=\:2.5\:m.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this case, the antennas aperture plays a fundamental role.
elements.sent-2411-067: As \blue{distance increases, \blue{the blocker relative dimensions are reduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: This favors the diffraction effects around the human~body~\blue{edges .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Finally, for the body orientation, median values of $-$51.5\:dB and $-$46.9\:dB \blue{are obtained in head-on and sideways crossings respectively; i.e. a difference of 4.6\:dB.}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, that is not the case for the blockage losses, which are usually described by the application of diffraction-based models as the~\ac{DTMKE \cite{Virk2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: They imply a high dependency on the geometrical description of the particular analyzed scenario, which translates into higher complexity.
elements.sent-2411-072: These are numerical factors that may derive in regression models, allowing us to describe the blockage \blue{gain along the wide spectrum of studied frequencies on the mmWave and sub-THz bands.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Seeking a simple description of the blockages behavior, we propose \blue{to model the BG with a modified version of the classical path loss models \cite{Rappaport2017 :.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Logarithmic and linear dependencies with frequency and distance\blue{, respectively, are assumed.}
elements.sent-2411-075: It is analogous to the \ac{CI model but \blue{introduces the intercept parameter \blue{$\beta$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: That is, \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_abg} \mathrm{\blue{BG}}(d,f) = \alpha d + \beta + 10\gamma\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:\mathrm{GHz}}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is the distance \blue{gain factor , and $\gamma$ is the frequency loss exponent.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then, \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_cif} \mathrm{\blue{BG}}(d,f) = a\left(1 + b\:\frac{f - f_0}{f_0}\right)d + 10c\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:GHz}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $a$ \blue{models the distance-dependent term , $b$ \blue{is the cross frequency-distance dependence \blue{factor , and $c$ is the frequency loss exponent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Additionally, $f_0$\:=\:145\:GHz \blue{corresponds to the central frequency of the \blue{modeled band.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: It is a zero-mean Gaussian distributed \ac{RV with variance~$\sigma^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: These are defined for frequencies up to 100\:GHz, but can be analytically extended to the upper bound of our range, i.e. up to 215\:GHz.
elements.sent-2411-081: The blockage gain estimation is based on \blue{modeling diffraction around metallic planes placed in a 3D space.}
elements.sent-2411-082: On the one hand, the 3GPP-A model adopts a stochastic approach \blue{and only needs to know the angles formed by the Rx and the edges of the planes.}
elements.sent-2411-083: On the other hand, the 3GPP-B model adopts a \blue{deterministic approach \blue{and all the distances between the edges of the metallic planes and both Tx and Rx are needed.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Then, the blockage gain can be computed as the sum of their individual contributions.
elements.sent-2411-085: Assuming a standard human, the dimensions of the body are a height $h$\:=\:1.7\:m, a width $r$\:=\:0.4\:m and a depth $w$\:=\:0.3\:m.
elements.sent-2411-086: The metallic planes (red and blue solid lines) are placed at both sides of the body, and their dimensions are of $w$\:$\times$\:$h$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The distance between the antennas is fixed to the corresponding value of $d$, and they are on a height $h_c$\:=\:1\:m above the ground.
elements.sent-2411-088: For the 3GPP-A model, \blue{we only need to compute the angles between the two paths at the receiver side.}
elements.sent-2411-089: However, for the 3GPP-B model, \blue{the full lengths of all the diffraction paths are required.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus, the optimal parameters are those which minimize the variance $\sigma^2$ of the stochastic term $\chi_\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-091: However, despite the high dispersion, it is able to properly capture the trend as Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq shows.}
elements.sent-2411-092: An intercept $A$\:=\:16.0\:dB and a loss exponent $n$\:=\:$-$3.09 are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-093: This fact enhances the relevance of human blockage effects at the sub-THz band.
elements.sent-2411-094: The minimum standard deviation $\sigma$ obtained for the model is $\sigma_{\rm FI}$\:=\:9.7\:dB, which is in line with \blue{the aforementioned dispersion seen in Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq .}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics{figures/new_figure5_v2.pdf} \caption{\blue{Comparison between the CI, ABG, and CIF models with the 3GPP-A and 3GPP-B estimations} for the three studied distances between the antennas.} \label{fig:freq_dist_models}
elements.sent-2411-095: The remaining models introduce the distance between the antennas \blue{as an additional variable to the operation frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-096: In the case of the CI model, as in the FI one, only two parameters are involved.
elements.sent-2411-097: After perform the fit, the obtained values are $\phi$\:=\:12.0\:dB/m for the distance gain factor and $m$\:=\:$-$3.32 for the frequency \blue{loss exponent.}
elements.sent-2411-098: For this model, the obtained parameters are a distance \blue{gain factor $\alpha$\:=\:12.1\:dB/m, an intercept $\beta$\:=\:$-$5.2\:dB, and a frequency \blue{loss exponent $\gamma$\:=\:$-$3.09.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, the CIF model modifies the CI one introducing a cross dependence with frequency and distance.
elements.sent-2411-100: The obtained parameters are a distance \blue{gain factor $a$\:=\:12.1\:dB/m, a cross frequency-distance factor $b$\:=\:0.0196, and a frequency \blue{loss exponent $c$\:=\:$-$3.32.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Regarding the parameter~$b$, it takes a reduced value which suggests that distance and frequency are uncorrelated.
elements.sent-2411-102: The regression curves are presented in Fig. \ref{fig:freq_dist_models , \blue{where it can be seen these models practically \blue{overlapping with each other.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Nevertheless, the three models properly capture the decreasing trend with frequency and the increasing trend with distance.
elements.sent-2411-104: They take a value \blue{near to 12\:dB/m, which is explained by the reduced wavelength at the sub-THz bands and the \blue{antennas' HPBW.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: An increase of one meter \blue{in distance involves moving the antennas away over several hundred wavelengths.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The obtained mean squared errors (MSEs) with respect to the measurements are $\mathrm{MSE _{\rm A}$}\:=\:8.9\:dB and \blue{$\mathrm{MSE _{\rm B}$}\:=\:9.0\:dB \blue{respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Both models results in similar BG values as can be seen in Fig. \ref{fig:freq_dist_models .}
elements.sent-2411-108: For a distance $d$\:=\:1\:m, the 3GPP models underestimate the \blue{blockage gain by about 10\:dB.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The difference \blue{becomes smaller as distance increases.}
elements.sent-2411-110: This behavior is explained attending to the Tx and Rx antennas, which are assumed isotropic by the 3GPP models.
elements.sent-2411-111: Under this condition, diffraction effects are favored in short-range compared to the high-directive antennas case.

elements.sent-2411-114: The loss exponent is clearly lower (in absolute terms) than for the fitted models, which suggests that \blue{the effects of blockages are more severe in the sub-THz frequencies than in the mmWave bands.}

elements.sent-2411-121: To the best of the authors' knowledge, this work has conducted for the first time a measurement campaign to study the effects of human blockage over an extremely wide band from 75\:GHz to\:215 GHz; including both mmWave and sub-THz frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-122: It has been seen that the extra attenuation due to humans in the range of 1\:m to 2.5\:m is between 42\:dB \blue{and 56\:dB, \blue{drastically affecting the \blue{received power.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: A fit with several models (FI, CI, ABG and, CIF) has been carried out to describe the blockage gain, obtaining in all cases a frequency loss exponent around 3 and a distance gain factor of 12\:dB/m.
elements.sent-2411-124: Finally, they have been compared with the standardized models 3GPP-A and 3GPP-B (based on site-specific geometry), which provide errors around 2.7\:dB higher.
elements.sent-2411-125: The obtained results are expected to be helpful for the development of D2D, BAN, and PAN communications systems.

elements.sent-2411-159: Measurements have been carried out in a laboratory at {the Universidad Politécnica de Cartagena~(UPCT), with {dimensions of 8\:$\times$\:4.8\:$\times$\:3.5\:m\textsuperscript{3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: The {environment is furnished with desks, chairs, shelves and closets; and it is equipped with several electronic devices such as computers and measurement instruments (see Fig. \ref{fig:setup_image ).}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Transmitter (Tx) and receiver~(Rx) are aligned {both horizontally and vertically, separated by a distance $d$\:$\in$\:\{1, 1.75, 2.5\}\:m and {placed at a height $h_c$\:=\:1\:m above the ground\blue{\footnote{\blue{Preliminary experimental tests suggest that the influence of antenna height is significantly lower compared to other factors such as frequency or the distance between the antennas.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: This setup resembles a device-to-device~(D2D) communication scenario within an indoor environment, where fading due to the transit of humans is typically observed.
elements.sent-2411-163: The channel sounder consists {in a vector network analyzer ({VNA, model R\&S\textsuperscript{\textregistered ZVA67) and several frequency converters ({models R\&S\textsuperscript{\textregistered ZVA-Z110E, ZC170 and ZC220){, enabling {signal acquisition in the range from 75\:GHz to 220\:GHz.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The Tx and Rx {use standard gain horn (SGH) {antennas with a gain of 20\:dB and a half-power beamwidth (HPBW) of 18º in {both the H and E planes at mid-band ({models Flann SGH Series 240 \#27240 and \#29240{, for the 75\:GHz--110\:GHz and 110\:GHz--215\:GHz bands respectively ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The VNA {operates in continuous wave (CW) mode, with a sampling frequency of 100\:Hz ({corresponding to a time step of 10\:ms).}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The number of points is set to 2\:048, which {yields a maximum excess time of 20.47\:s.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Nine different frequencies are evaluated, {equispaced by 17.5\:GHz in the range from 75\:GHz to 215\:GHz.}
elements.sent-2411-168: The most restrictive dynamic range is above 80\:dB, and it is observed for the highest frequency.
elements.sent-2411-169: Additionally, the distances are large enough to assume that the variation of the antennas HPBW with frequency have no significant impact on the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-170: Two subjects participated in the {experiment , performing {2 head-on and 2 sideways crossings per frequency and distance.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Therefore, the total number of captured blockages {events is {2\:$\times$\:4\:$\times$\:9\:$\times$\:3\:=\: 216.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: This diversity, along with the different crossings, frequencies, and distances, allows for the generation of several attenuation patterns.

elements.sent-2411-173: The {signal magnitude is normalized to the power of the LoS component (including antenna gain{s ), {resulting in the continuous 0\:dB value observed at the beginning and end of the {trace .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Additionally, several {phenomena such as arm movements ({which cause a partial signal recovery) {as well as diffraction plus reflection interferences are {also observed.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: This can be done {by averaging over the local mean power with a {sliding window of 1 6 samples\footnote{Given a crossing speed of 45\:cm/s, {this window size is equivalent to 32$\lambda$ at the midband frequency.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Then, the concept of blockage gain (BG) naturally arises.
elements.sent-2411-177: We define it as the minimum gain {observed along the blockage after removing the fast fading effects.}
elements.sent-2411-178: In {the example shown in Fig. \ref{fig:blockage_example , {the blockage gain takes a value of $-$45.5\:dB.}}}

elements.section-2411-005: Analysis and Modeling of Blockage Gain
elements.sent-2411-179: Applying the aforementioned definition, the BG parameter {was computed for each captured blockage event.}
elements.sent-2411-180: As {shown in Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq {(a) , the BG {exhibits a clear {inversely proportional relation with frequency.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: In particular, median values of $-$43.0\:dB and $-$59.7\:dB are {obtained at the lowest and highest frequencies respectively (75\:GHz and 215\:GHz).}
elements.sent-2411-182: While the {free-space signal attenuation {along this frequency {band ranges 9.1\:dB, {additional losses of up to 16.7\:dB are observed during the blockage event{s .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: This is due to the {growing electrical dimensions of the blocker {at higher frequencies , which {increases the signal attenuation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: In contrast to the frequency trend, the blockage gain becomes more noticeable as the distance between the antennas becomes shorter.
elements.sent-2411-185: As {the distance increases, the relative dimensions {of the blocker with respect to the antenna beam decreases, enhancing the diffraction around the {edges of the human~body.On the other hand, in terms of the human subject, the discrepancy between the median {BG values is 7.2\:dB, {indicating clear differences in their {physical constitutions.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Finally, {regarding the body orientation, median values of $-$51.5\:dB and $-$46.9\:dB are obtained {for head-on and sideways crossings respectively{, yielding a difference of 4.6\:dB.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: This {result highlights the impact of the {human body dimensions along the LoS direction, which is higher in the head-on case than in the sideways case.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: From this, $p$-values lower than 0.01 were obtained for all frequency, distance between the antennas, human subject and blocker orientation; which implies that these are significant at the 99\%.
elements.sent-2411-189: In the state-of-the-art, there {are numerous proposals for modeling path loss as a simple function of frequency and distance.}
elements.sent-2411-190: However, {this is not the case for the blockage losses, which are usually described by diffraction-based models as the~\ac{DTMKE \cite{Virk2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: As mentioned in previous paragraphs, blockage gain {exhibits a clear dependence on frequency and distance.}
elements.sent-2411-192: These numerical factors {can be incorporated into regression models, allowing us to describe the blockage gain {across the wide {range of studied frequencies on the mmWave and sub-THz bands.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Seeking a simple description of the blockage behavior, we propose {modeling the BG {using modified version{s of the classical path loss models \cite{Rappaport2017 :.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Blockage gain {depends logarithmically on frequency, and it is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_fi} \mathrm{BG}(f) = A + 10n\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:\mathrm{GHz}}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $A$ is the intercept\footnote{{The intercept parameter represents the blockage gain value returned by the model at the frequency and distance of reference.}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: In this case, for $f$\:=\:1\:GHz and for a distance $d$\:=\:0.
elements.sent-2411-196: It can be interpreted as a bias or offset in the model.
elements.sent-2411-197: Logarithmic and linear dependencies with frequency and distance, respectively, are assumed.
elements.sent-2411-198: Then, \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_ci} \mathrm{BG}(d,f) = \phi d + 10m\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:\mathrm{GHz}}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $\phi$ models the distance-dependent term, and $m$ represents the frequency loss exponent.
elements.sent-2411-199: That is, \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_abg} \mathrm{BG}(d,f) = \alpha d + \beta + 10\gamma\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:\mathrm{GHz}}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is the distance gain factor, and $\gamma$ is the frequency loss exponent.
elements.sent-2411-200: This model introduces a cross frequency-distance dependence.
elements.sent-2411-201: Then, \begin{equation}\label{eq:human_blockage_cif} BG(d,f) = a\left(1 + b\:\frac{f - f_0}{f_0}\right)d + 10c\log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{1\:GHz}\right) + \chi_\sigma,\end{equation} where $a$ models the distance-dependent term, $b$ is the cross frequency-distance dependence factor, and $c$ is the frequency loss exponent.
elements.sent-2411-202: Additionally, $f_0$\:=\:145\:GHz {is the central frequency of the {studied band.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: All the models {expressions are in logarithmic scale (i.e., units of decibels) and include a stochastic term $\chi_\sigma$ to capture factors not explicitly {modeled such as blocker orientation, human subject {differences , and other masked influences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: It is {assumed to be a zero-mean Gaussian distributed \ac{RV with variance~$\sigma^2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: The main differences compared to the classical path-loss models are \blue{(i) substitution of logarithmic dependence on distance by a linear one, and (ii) replacing the free-space path loss reference by a one-freedom degree power law in frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-206: These changes allow us to describe the blockage effects in a straightforward manner using a reduced number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-207: Nevertheless, for locations closer to the Tx/Rx antenna, they return a lower bound of BG by considering an equivalent distance $d$ equal to twice the distance from the blocker to the closest antenna.
elements.sent-2411-208: In order to {compare our proposed models with geometry-based alternatives, we also apply the 3GPP blockage models 3GPP-A and 3GPP-B \cite{3GPP2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The blockage gain estimation is based on modeling diffraction around metallic planes placed in a 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-210: For our {specific case, we represent the human blocker as two metallic planes whose height and width are adjusted to {match the dimensions of the {human body.}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: The blockage gain {is then computed as the sum of {the individual contributions {from each plane .}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Assuming a {typical human, the {body dimensions are a height $h$\:=\:1.7\:m, a width $r$\:=\:0.4\:m, and a depth $w$\:=\:0.3\:m.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: As {illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:human_blocking_scheme , the blocker {is positioned at the midpoint of the Tx--Rx line, where the attenuation is maximum.}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: The metallic planes ({shown as red and blue solid lines) are placed {on either side of the body, {each with dimensions $w$\:$\times$\:$h$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: The distance between the antennas is {set to the corresponding value of $d$, and {both are {located at a height $h_c$\:=\:1\:m above the ground.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The geometry {required to estimate the blockage gain is defined by the diffraction paths {indicated by the red and blue dashed lines.}}
elements.sent-2411-217: For the 3GPP-A model, only the angles {formed by these paths at the receiver side are needed.}
elements.sent-2411-218: However, the 3GPP-B model {requires the full lengths of all the diffraction paths {at both transmitter and receiver sides.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: In both cases, these geometric features are entirely determined by the blocker's dimensions and the Tx-Rx distance.
elements.sent-2411-220: A fit between the models presented in eqs.
elements.sent-2411-221: The FI model is the simplest {among those considered, since it only account{s for the operation frequency.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Despite the high dispersion {in measurements , it {successfully capture{s the {observed trend as {shown in Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: An intercept $A$\:=\:16.0\:dB and a loss exponent $n$\:=\:$-$3.09 {were obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-224: These results {indicate that {a frequency increment in the order of 10 leads to a reduction of approximately 30\:dB approximately on the received {power .}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: This {highlights the {significance of human blockage effects at the sub-THz band.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: At these frequencies, the electrical dimensions of obstacles increase {substantially compared to those at sub-6\:GHz and mmWave bands, {resulting in higher penetration losses and {a reduced impact of diffraction mechanisms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: The minimum standard deviation $\sigma$ obtained for the model is $\sigma_{\rm FI}$\:=\:9.7\:dB, which {aligns with the aforementioned dispersion seen in Fig. \ref{fig:boxplot_freq .}}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.79\linewidth]{figures/fig5.pdf} \caption{Comparison between the CI, ABG, and CIF {blockage gain fitting models (blue, red and yellow lines)} with the 3GPP-A and 3GPP-B {blockage gain geometry-based models (purple and green lines)} for the three studied distances between the antennas. {The blue and purple points represent the empirical samples of blockage gain obtained from head-on and sideways crossings respectively. From the zoomed-in views of the curves, it can be observed that the three fitting models closely overlap with each other, as well as the two geometrical models. Additionally, since blockage gain is inversely related to attenuation, the figure clearly illustrates that shorter Tx-Rx distances $d$ are tailored to higher attenuation while longer distances $d$ are associated with lower attenuation.}} \label{fig:freq_dist_models}
elements.sent-2411-228: The remaining models introduce the distance between the antennas as an additional variable {alongside the operation frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-229: In the case of the CI model, as {with the FI one, only two parameters are involved.}
elements.sent-2411-230: After {performing the fit, the obtained values {were $\phi$\:=\:12.0\:dB/m for the distance gain factor and $m$\:=\:$-$3.32 for the frequency loss exponent.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: This last parameter is consistent with that obtained for the FI model but {is slightly higher due to the absence of an intercept parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-232: When {the intercept is introduced, the ABG model naturally emerges as a generalization of the CI {model .}}
elements.sent-2411-233: For this model, the obtained parameters {were a distance gain factor $\alpha$\:=\:12.1\:dB/m, an intercept $\beta$\:=\:$-$5.2\:dB, and a frequency loss exponent $\gamma$\:=\:$-$3.09.}
elements.sent-2411-234: The obtained parameters {were a distance gain factor $a$\:=\:12.1\:dB/m, a cross frequency-distance factor $b$\:=\:0.0196, and a frequency loss exponent $c$\:=\:$-$3.32.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Since there is no intercept parameter, $c$ is equal to that {of the CI model.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Regarding parameter~$b$, {its small value suggests that distance and frequency are uncorrelated {in this scenario, which explains the {similarity between the {models parameters .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-242: Another {important observation is the {consistency between the distance gain factors $\phi$, $\alpha$, and $a$ {in the CI, ABG and CIF {models respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: They {converge to a value near to 12\:dB/m, which is explained by the reduced wavelength at the sub-THz bands and the antennas' HPBW.}
elements.sent-2411-244: An increase of one meter in distance {means separating the antennas {by several hundred wavelengths, significantly {reducing the relative electrical dimensions of the blocker {and thus favoring the diffraction mechanisms around its~edges.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: After post-processing, the root mean squared errors (RMSEs) obtained for the FI, CI, ABG and CIF models were 9.48\:dB, 6.01\:dB, 5.89\:dB and 6.13\:dB respectively.
elements.sent-2411-246: In all cases, these values are lower than the corresponding standard deviations, indicating that the blockage gain measurements for the third person are within the variability range of the models.

elements.sent-2411-258: Finally, all the results obtained from fitting and estimating the blockage gain are summarized in Table \ref{tab:numerical_results .}
elements.sent-2411-259: For our site-specific conditions, the frequency loss exponent is {consistently above 30\:dB/decade, {while the distance gain factor is {approximately 12\:dB/m.}}}

elements.sent-2411-265: The inclusion of human blockage effects in next-generation channel models is essential to accurately predict the wireless propagation behavior, {particularly at the sub-THz {frequencies .}}
elements.sent-2411-266: To the best of the authors' knowledge, this work has conducted for the first time a measurement campaign {analyzing human blockage over an extremely wide band, from 75\:GHz to 215\:GHz, {encompassing both mmWave and sub-THz frequencies.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Several models (FI, CI, ABG and, CIF) {were fitted to the experimental values of blockage gain, {consistently yielding a frequency loss exponent {of approximately $-$3 and distance gain factor of 12\:dB/m.}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: The {findings of this work are expected to {aid in the development of D2D, BAN, and PAN communications systems {by incorporating blockage gain as an additional term in the link-budget evaluations .}}}
